paragraphs: Home. [#pause]Pay attention to the stew. [#pause]Pay attention to the kid. [#pause]Joana wasn't. [#pause]She was doing the math.

sections.heading: HAB-PERERA · FREYA HOMESTEAD
paragraphs: At her desk in the hab unit's corner, Joana studied EEG waveforms. It was the alphabet of the soul, translated into spikes and valleys. She scrolled through archives, cross-referencing Fold transit data with old Earth psilocybin studies. The correlations were stark yet completely insufficient. Her hab was warm and smelled of the stew that Ian had made her for lunch. It had gone cold, a skin of orange fat forming over the surface. From the rug behind her, [#clickclack]little Susana was quietly building a gravity-defying mag-block tower.

Susana's brain scan showed radical plasticity that filled Joana with clinical awe. The mechanism was well-established: 5-HT2A receptor agonism led to a marked decrease in what Joana referred to as the mind's autopilot, the Default Mode Network, the signature of ego dissolution. She had observed similar results in dimethyltryptamine studies. The Fold transit, however, was different. Yet she couldn't quite define it.

Joana reviewed the logs she had collected from the crew, comparing them with her own Fold transit experience. She'd tried to peek through Ian's too. But sadly, he had never filed one. His baseline readings were flagged as corrupted. She peered through other accounts that described complex, vivid imagery and profound cognitive and emotional shifts.

paragraphs.text: "There are levels to it," one person had written. "At first, I heard a hum. I couldn't quite place it, but it caused my body to relax completely. Colors became… more themselves. I felt weightless and embraced. A geometric matrix appeared, revealing a universal architecture behind everything."
Another report, from a young astrogator: "I entered a hyperspace jungle, seeing colors and shapes I can't describe. There was a sense of oneness with everything."

paragraphs: Joana closed her eyes, recalling her own experience. She remembered traveling through a river of swirling paint, leading her through a kaleidoscopic cathedral. Within, she had touched the fabric of space, a syntax of light and color beyond her comprehension.
She opened her eyes and scrolled further. One ensign described the ringing of an intricate bell. She remembered studying a compass, with a needle spinning endlessly.

A flagged trend in the aggregate data drew her attention to the present. It showed a progressive dampening of limbic system activity across most of the Homestead's adult population. The fluctuations of communal emotion—anxiety, frustration, and joy—were being leveled. No other explanation fit. She was observing the direct effects of a large-scale introduction of neuroregulators into the life-support system.

On a scientific level, she could admire the Grid's elegance, yet she felt complicit in its creation. Her neurochemical research into their well-being and her detailed mapping of their neural landscapes had provided the foundation for the Grid's calibration. And it was her Ian, out there, turning that blueprint into reality, conduit by conduit, hab by hab.
Anxiety about the Unfolding was spreading through the Homestead. They'd all become orphaned from Mother Earth.

The fans' whirring faded. Even the click-clack of Susana's blocks had stopped. Joana turned and found Susana stiff on the rug, eyes vacant. [#gutteral]A guttural sound escaped Su's throat. Joana sprang from the chair, crossing over to her in big strides. The wall-mounted Grid [#chime2]interface chimed.
< ALERT | HAB GAMMA-ECHO 9
TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE DETECTED.
OCCUPANT: SUSANA PERERA. PEDIATRIC. />

A storm was tearing through Su's tiny body. Tonic. Clonic. Clinical words for a daughter turning into a vibrating wreck.[#pause] Joana knelt beside her daughter as her small body began to convulse. She rolled Susana onto her side to keep her airway clear and shoved her thigh under Susana's head to stop her skull from cracking against the floor.
The [#airhiss]ceiling vent hissed. Joana smelled something bitter in the air.

A deep tiredness washed over her, making her arms and legs feel heavy. But Susana's tremors stopped at once. The wild energy was gone. Her body went limp like a puppet whose strings had been cut. [#pause]Joana placed two fingers on Susana's neck. Her pulse was slow but steady.
<[#chime2] INTERVENTION SUCCESSFUL.
NEUROSTABILITY ACHIEVED. />
Joana gathered her limp daughter into her arms and held her close. Her own hands shook. [#pause]Exhausted, she began to cry.
The [#doorhiss3]door slid open, and the hab exhaled. Ian entered, weary from a long shift. He had hurried home, his lungs burning.

paragraphs.text: "[crying]The Grid intervened again." Joana sighed and wiped away her tears.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: He [#mugslide]put his case on the counter. "Good. That's a good thing, Jo."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[frustrated]No! If it keeps doing this, she'll keep having them. It'll get worse. Then what?"
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: Ian ran his hand through his hair. "We'll figure it out."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[frustrated]I can help her through the seizures. It's the only way she'll recover. The Grid isn't helping, Ian. [pause][sigh][accusatory]You're out there connecting all the habs to a chemical factory."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: Ian checked his overalls, his hands trembling slightly. "[mutters]My job," he muttered, "[mutters]is to keep us safe. Breathing. Sane. Fed."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: He glanced at the empty shelves in the kitchen. "[voice breaks][upset]It can't keep sedating her!" she shot back.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: Ian's eyes were bloodshot, the whites turned to a map of red rivers. "[disbelieving]Do you really want to risk it?!" He clenched his fists. They knew that a long seizure could cause brain damage from a lack of oxygen.
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs: < [#chime2]WARNING | EMOTIONAL VOLATILITY DETECTED.
RECOMMEND REGULATION. />
Ian's anger faded as he looked from Joana's pleading face to Susana, who lay quietly in her arms.

paragraphs.text: "[desperate]This will get worse," Joana said. Her teeth were bared, a white line of bone. "You know it."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: A silence fell between them. Ian turned away without a word and went into the bathroom. He braced himself on the sink. The long shift had worn him out. Now this. A compulsive tic started at the corner of his eye, and his breath hitched. *[panicked]Not now. Not here.* He saw himself double. Two Ians. One wiring the world, one entangled inside it.

paragraphs: < [#chime2]WARNING | ANXIETY EXCEEDING LIMIT. />

paragraphs.text: The small vent above the [#airhiss]mirror hissed. "[rasp]No," he rasped. He grabbed his slate and [#slam]slammed it against the [#slam]mirror, again and [#slam]again, until the mirror [#glassbreak]broke. Ian blacked out, fell to his knees, and [#glassbreak2]slid through the broken glass.
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: The [#doorburst2]door burst open. Joana hurried in. "[shout]Ian!" she screamed.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs.text: Kneeling beside him, she pulled him away from the worst of the glass. "[mumbles]It'll be okay," he mumbled.
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[crying]I know," Joana replied as she checked the cuts on his forearm. "[groans][under breath]We'll get through this."[pause]
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera
